locked_facts: Pressure = Force/Area: P = F/A (scalar quantity, acts in all directions)
Pascal's law: pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions
Hydraulic lift: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂ → F₂ = F₁(A₂/A₁) — force amplification
Pressure at depth h in a fluid: P = P₀ + ρgh
Atmospheric pressure at sea level: P₀ ≈ 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa
Pressure at a point in a fluid at rest is the same in all directions
Hydraulic systems conserve energy: work in = work out (F₁d₁ = F₂d₂)
truth_statements: Pascal's law states that a change in pressure at any point in an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally to all points in the fluid
A hydraulic lift multiplies force but not work — the small piston moves a larger distance to compensate
Pressure in a fluid at rest depends only on depth, not on the shape of the container
The pressure at the bottom of interconnected vessels of any shape is the same at the same horizontal level
Gauge pressure is the pressure above atmospheric: P_gauge = ρgh

aha_moment.what_to_show: A hydraulic lift: small force F₁ on small piston → transmitted through fluid → large force F₂ on large piston. But the small piston moves far while the large piston moves little. Work = Fd is the same on both sides — force is amplified, not energy.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Small piston moving far, large piston moving little, with Fd products equal on both sides
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think hydraulic lifts create free force — seeing the distance trade-off makes energy conservation obvious

simulation_states.label: Pressure basics
simulation_states.description: Force applied on fluid through a piston. P = F/A. Same force on smaller area → higher pressure. Pressure acts equally in all directions.
simulation_states.emphasis: Pressure = F/A, and it acts in all directions inside the fluid

simulation_states.label: Pascal's law
simulation_states.description: Enclosed fluid with two connected cylinders. Pressure applied to small piston transmitted to large piston. F₂ = F₁(A₂/A₁). Force amplification demonstrated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same pressure everywhere → larger area gets larger force

simulation_states.label: Energy conservation
simulation_states.description: Small piston pushed down distance d₁. Large piston rises distance d₂ = d₁(A₁/A₂). Work: F₁d₁ = F₂d₂. Force gained, distance lost. No free energy.
simulation_states.emphasis: Force multiplied but distance divided — work is CONSERVED

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts piston areas and input force. Output force, displacement, and work calculated. Pressure gauges at multiple points show equal pressure. Depth-pressure demo.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change area ratio — force amplification changes but work stays constant

common_misconceptions.belief: Hydraulic systems create energy by amplifying force
common_misconceptions.correction: Hydraulic systems amplify force but reduce displacement proportionally. Work = Force × Distance is conserved. If force is multiplied by 10, the large piston moves 1/10 the distance.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show F₁d₁ = F₂d₂ with animated distance comparison

common_misconceptions.belief: Pressure at the bottom of a container depends on the container's shape
common_misconceptions.correction: Pressure at depth h depends only on ρgh + P₀, not on the container shape. A thin tube and a wide tank at the same depth have the same pressure at the bottom (hydrostatic paradox).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show different shaped containers with same depth — pressure gauges at bottom all read the same
